skill.name: foundations-problem-solution-fit
skill.description: Guides product teams through problem validation, solution hypothesis evaluation, alternative analysis, MVP scoping, and innovation strategy.
skill.summary: Validate customer problems and shape MVP scope before major product investment.
skill.author: BellaBe

security_audit.summary: The static analyzer reported command execution, weak cryptography, and reconnaissance patterns, but review found these are false positives in Markdown templates and product strategy text. The skill contains guidance, JSON examples, and output formats only; no executable scripts, network calls, credential access, or prompt injection language were found.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Markdown Code Fences Flagged as Shell Execution
low_findings.description: The reported Ruby or shell backtick execution locations are Markdown code fences around output templates and JSON examples. They do not instruct the assistant to execute commands and do not contain shell code.
low_findings.recommendation: No remediation is required for security. Keep fenced examples clearly marked as templates or JSON.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed locations are fenced Markdown examples, not executable code. No command interpreter, shell syntax, or user-controlled execution path is present.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Business Terms Flagged as Weak Cryptography
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography matches occur in ordinary words and product planning phrases, such as description fields, critical assumptions, and platform strategy. No cryptographic algorithm, hashing function, encryption library, or security implementation appears in the skill.
low_findings.recommendation: No remediation is required. These lines are product strategy language, not cryptographic code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review found no MD5, SHA1, DES, RC4, or crypto API use. The hits are semantic false positives caused by common words in a non-code document.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Interview Prompts Flagged as Reconnaissance
low_findings.description: The reconnaissance matches are customer discovery and product strategy prompts. They ask about user workflows, unmet needs, and product pitfalls, not host information, environment variables, network topology, or local system details.
low_findings.recommendation: No remediation is required. The content is normal customer research guidance.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed lines are natural-language product discovery content. I found no instructions to enumerate files, inspect hosts, collect credentials, or probe systems.

content.value_statement: Teams often build products before proving that the customer problem is urgent and valuable. This skill guides structured discovery, solution comparison, MVP scoping, and defensibility analysis.
actual_capabilities: Ranks customer problems by frequency, intensity, evidence, solvability, and strategic fit.
Compares solution concepts by feasibility, effort, expected impact, and validation criteria.
Analyzes direct competitors, indirect alternatives, workarounds, and switching barriers.
Defines MVP scope with core features, non-features, dependencies, metrics, and pivot triggers.
Frames innovation strategy around 10x improvement, unique insight, defensibility, and platform potential.
limitations: Does not conduct interviews, surveys, or market research by itself.
Does not verify competitor data unless the user provides evidence or asks for research.
Does not replace product, legal, financial, or technical due diligence.
Works best when users provide segment details, interview notes, constraints, and existing alternatives.

use_cases.title: Validate a Startup Idea
use_cases.description: Turn early customer notes into a ranked problem list, solution hypotheses, and first validation experiments.

use_cases.title: Scope an MVP
use_cases.description: Translate validated problems into core features, explicit non-features, development effort, metrics, and pivot triggers.

use_cases.title: Assess a Product Pivot
use_cases.description: Compare weak traction signals against customer pain, alternatives, and possible solution changes before changing direction.

prompt_templates.title: Rank Customer Problems
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the foundations-problem-solution-fit skill to rank these customer problems. Include frequency, intensity, evidence quality, solvability, and a recommended focus.

prompt_templates.title: Compare Solution Concepts
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the foundations-problem-solution-fit skill to evaluate these solution concepts for one validated problem. Compare feasibility, effort, impact, differentiation, and validation criteria.

prompt_templates.title: Define MVP Scope
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the foundations-problem-solution-fit skill to create an MVP specification. Include core features, nice-to-haves, non-features, dependencies, success metrics, and pivot triggers.

prompt_templates.title: Pressure-Test Defensibility
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the foundations-problem-solution-fit skill to assess whether this product can become 10x better than alternatives. Include unique insight, moats, network effects, and validation steps.

output_examples.input: We interviewed 12 operations managers about manual invoice reconciliation.
output_examples.output: A ranked problem stack with evidence strength, severity scores, solvability notes, and one recommended problem to validate next.

output_examples.input: We have three product concepts for reducing onboarding drop-off.
output_examples.output: A solution comparison that highlights feasibility, effort, expected impact, prototype approach, and the strongest validation criteria.

output_examples.input: Our MVP keeps expanding and the team cannot agree on launch scope.
output_examples.output: A focused MVP plan with must-have features, explicit non-features, timeline risks, success metrics, and pivot triggers.

best_practices: Provide real interview quotes, observed behavior, and quantified pain whenever possible.
Ask for multiple solution options before selecting an MVP direction.
Define success metrics and pivot triggers before building the first version.
anti_patterns: Using the skill to justify a solution when the problem has not been validated.
Treating output templates as facts without customer evidence.
Expanding MVP scope with features that do not solve the primary problem.

faq.question: What does this skill help me decide?
faq.answer: It helps decide which customer problem to solve, which solution approach to test, and what belongs in the MVP.

faq.question: Does it perform market research automatically?
faq.answer: No. It organizes and evaluates evidence that you provide, unless you separately ask your AI tool to research sources.

faq.question: Can it compare competitors and alternatives?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes direct competitors, indirect alternatives, workarounds, non-consumption, switching barriers, and unmet needs.

faq.question: Is it useful after a product has launched?
faq.answer: Yes. It can support pivot assessment, feature pruning, and problem revalidation when traction is weak.

faq.question: What input should I provide?
faq.answer: Provide customer segments, problem hypotheses, interview notes, usage data, competitor names, team constraints, and timeline limits.

faq.question: Does it produce engineering tickets?
faq.answer: It can shape MVP features and user stories, but detailed engineering tickets should be reviewed by the product and engineering team.
